title: Anger at God for the Death of a Friend
dek: You clutch the ache in your chest, the rawness beneath your ribs, the heat pooling behind your eyes. This is not weakness; it is the body’s way of holding grief and fury in the same trembling space. When a friend dies, and your fists clench against the heavens, it’s not blasphemy - it’s biology. You are learning to listen to your body’s protest, the question pounding in the masseter and the diaphragm: Why? Why was this taken from me?
bodyMarkdown:
You wake to the weight of the night pressing down on your chest, the kind of pressure that makes your ribs ache as if they’ve been strapped tight in a too-small corset. It’s 3:17 a.m., and the air in your room smells faintly of chamomile - steeped and forgotten on the nightstand, a pale yellow reminder of last night’s attempt at calm. But calm is a lie now. The news of your friend’s death crashes into you like a wave, not cold water but molten iron spilling through your veins. Anger roars in your body, a furious, raw thing that tastes metallic on your tongue and makes your teeth clench so hard the masseter muscle above your jawbone throbs with pain. It’s a betrayal so profound you want to scream at the heavens, at God, at the silence that swallowed her whole.


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.169Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

This is not simple grief. This is a body remembering a wound it wasn’t ready to carry. Your trapezius tightens, the fibers knotting between your shoulder blades, as if your body is trying to hold itself together against an invisible force pulling it apart. You feel the levator scapulae stiffen, a sharp ache running from the base of your skull to the top of your shoulders, and somewhere deep, the diaphragm spasms - your breath catches, uneven and shallow. You want to breathe fully, but the betrayed part of you says no, holds your lungs hostage in a cage of disbelief and fury.

Karla McLaren, who has taught so many of us to listen to the language of anger, would say this is your body’s messenger - this rage that feels like it might consume you is the voice of a boundary shattered. Anger is not your enemy; it is the sentinel that signals harm done, the body’s way of saying, “This is not right.” And what is more not right than the universe taking someone you loved without warning, without justice? Your anger is the echo of that rupture. It is biology, not madness. The muscles remember what your mind struggles to name.

Gabor Maté, whose work you’ve read in stolen moments between life’s demands, reminds us that trauma is not only what happens to us but what happens inside us afterward. The betrayal you feel is a trauma of faith, a rupture in your internal landscape where trust once lived. Maté’s words settle in your chest like a balm and a challenge: “The body keeps the score.” Your body isn’t just recording the loss - it’s holding the outrage, the disbelief, the raw, ragged edges of your faith unraveling. The psoas muscle, that deep core muscle where so much fear and trauma lodge, clenches involuntarily, a silent scream in the architecture of your body.

You might catch yourself wanting to curse God right now, to demand answers that will never come. The betrayal is so sharp that it tastes like acid on your tongue. You want to shake the heavens and say, “How could you? How could you let her go?” But the silence answers back, a void that presses into your ears and fills your lungs with heaviness. This is the body’s way of mourning what the mind cannot grasp. The jaw remembers every unspoken word, every hug that won’t be given, every laugh that will never echo again.

Outside, the wind picks up, rattling the window frame, a cold gust that snakes down your spine. There is no comfort in this night, only the relentless pulse of grief and the storm of anger tangled together like weeds around your heart. Your hands tremble, fingers curling into fists, knuckles white against the soft cotton of your sheets. You reach blindly for the mug of nettle tea on the floor, its earthiness grounding you for a moment. Nettles, for their blood-cleansing and heart-opening properties, are a medicine that you trust to steady the chaos inside. The herbs do not promise to fix this - they never do - but they remind your body that it is supported, that the earth still holds you even when the sky feels empty.

You remember a teaching from Pema Chödrön about sitting with what hurts without running away. “The pain will teach you,” she says, “if you don’t run from it.” But this pain is so vast, so encompassing, that you can barely sit with it. It spreads through you like wildfire, scorching the fascia that wraps around your organs, tightening the connective tissue until it feels like your insides are being squeezed through a narrow funnel. Your whole torso feels constricted, as if your body is trying to hold onto something slipping away beyond your grasp.

The anger at God is not blasphemy here - it is honesty. It is the fierce refusal to accept the silence, the absence, the terrible unfairness of death. It is the body screaming in the language it knows best: tension, heat, constriction, the hot flush in your cheeks and the icy cold in your fingertips. This fury is not ugly; it is the raw, unfiltered language of loss. The muscles remember what the mind cannot yet say, and the body insists on being heard.

You think of Resmaa Menakem’s teachings on racialized trauma and how trauma lives in the body’s extremities - the hands, the feet, the face. This anger pulses there, too, in your clenched fists and the tight set of your jaw. It is the body’s rebellion against the injustice of death and the silence of divinity. This is not weakness; it is fierce survival.

You may find yourself pacing the room, the wooden floor cool beneath your bare feet, each step a desperate attempt to move the energy that feels stuck in your core. The levator scapulae and trapezius protest with a sharp ache, each movement a reminder that your body is not separate from your grief. It carries the story in every twitch and spasm, in every breath that catches and every sigh that escapes into the night.

This is the beginning of a long conversation with your body and your anger. It is the first tremor after the earthquake - a shaking that cannot be silenced or smoothed over with platitudes. Your body demands a reckoning, a naming of the rage that rises from the depths like a tidal wave. It is the betrayal felt in every fiber of your being, the fierce, unyielding truth of loss.

And so, you sit with it. You breathe into the diaphragm that spasms with each breathless moment. You soften the grip of the masseter when the jaw clenches in defiance. You reach for the nettle, the chamomile, the ginger - herbs that speak the language of resilience, plants that root you back in the flesh of the world. This is not a moment for answers. This is a moment for presence, for listening to the body’s truth when words fail.

You are not alone in this. The great teachers like Karla McLaren and Gabor Maté have walked this terrain before, mapping the wild landscape of anger and trauma. They remind you that the body knows what the mind cannot yet understand. Your anger at God is a prayer in its own right - a fierce, trembling demand to be seen, to be held, to be answered in the language only your body can speak.

The night stretches on, heavy and unyielding. It’s 4:02 a.m., and the first pale light of dawn presses faintly against your curtains, but your body refuses the comfort of morning. Your trapezius still clenches like a vise, the fibers stiff and protesting between your shoulder blades. The levator scapulae screams in protest every time you tilt your head, a sharp reminder that your body is still caught in the grip of betrayal. The diaphragm spasms, shallow breaths hitching in your throat, and the masseter muscle tightens again, the jaw locked in a defiant clench that no amount of gentle coaxing can fully soften.

You reach for the Motherwort tincture you prepared weeks ago, the bittersweet scent of the herb rising like a balm from the small glass bottle. Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) is a medicine of the heart, a nervine and mild relaxant prized in Traditional Chinese Medicine and Western herbalism alike for its ability to ease the tightness in the chest and soothe the wild, stormy heart. It isn’t a cure, and it won’t stop the ache, but it eases the constriction there, softening the fascia wrapped tight around your lungs and ribs like a too-tight garment. You take a slow sip, the bitters anchoring you in the moment. This is the body remembering how to breathe again, one trembling breath at a time.

Hawthorn berries, steeped into a deep red tea, wait beside the Motherwort. Hawthorn (Crataegus monogyna) is the ancient heart tonic, a conversation partner for your body’s grief. It tells your heart, “I see you; I hold you.” Hawthorn’s bittersweet flesh and thorny branches remind you that the heart is both vulnerable and fiercely protected, capable of breaking and still beating on. The flavonoids in hawthorn calm the nervous system, slow a racing heart, and open vascular pathways, whispering to your blood vessels to relax and your psoas muscle to unclench its silent scream. You drink deeply, feeling the warmth spread from your chest through your core, a slow loosening of the grip that grief holds on your body.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora), with its delicate purple flowers, waits in a jar, its nervine properties known to soothe the restless mind and ease muscle tension. The masseter muscle, still throbbing from clenched teeth, finds a tender ally in skullcap. You steep the herb, the slightly bitter, earthy aroma filling the room, and as you drink, the tension in your jaw begins to ebb, the tight fascia wrapping your temples and cheeks softening imperceptibly. The skullcap is medicine for the body’s battlefields - the muscles locked in defense, the nerves on edge, the spirit ragged from the storm.

Pema Chödrön’s voice surfaces in the quiet between your breaths, a guide tethering you to the rawness. She insists that sitting with the pain - the terrible, unyielding, and often unbearable pain - is the only way through. “The pain will teach you if you don’t run from it,” she says. But you resist the invitation; the pain is a wildfire burning through your chest, scorching the diaphragm and tightening the connective tissue until it feels like your body is being squeezed through a narrow funnel. Still, you lean in, because you know the body knows. The diaphragm spasms are not punishment but a language, a call for presence in a moment that feels like it will swallow you whole.

Bessel van der Kolk’s work threads through your mind like a lifeline. The body keeps the score, yes - but it also carries the memory of safety lost and the urgent need to find it again. Van der Kolk teaches that trauma embeds not just in the mind but in the fascia, the muscles, the breath, the very architecture of the body. Your psoas muscle clenches, a reflexive coil of protection, anchoring your fear and rage deep in your core. You sense how the trauma of loss is layered into the tissues, how the body’s survival response has turned inward, holding you captive in a cage of tension and disbelief.

You move to the window, the chill of early morning pressing through the glass, the air sharp with the scent of wet earth and pine. Your feet find the cold wooden floor, grounding you in the raw reality of the body’s response. You feel the muscles in your calves twitch, the arches of your feet flexing as if searching for purchase on this fractured ground. The hands - those hands that clench so fiercely - relax just enough to brush against the sill. The skin smells faintly of nettle and sweat, a mix that reminds you you are alive, raw and exposed but breathing.

You remember Resmaa Menakem’s teaching about trauma living in the extremities - the hands, feet, face - and how those places hold the rage and sorrow that words cannot reach. Your hands, still curled into fists, pulse with a cold fire. You press your palms flat against the window, fingers splayed wide, and breathe into the tension pooled in your trapezius and levator scapulae. You feel the stubborn, knotted fibers begin to quiver under your touch, the fascia slowly unwinding its grip.

Motherwort, hawthorn, skullcap - they are not magic, but they are medicine. They sit with you in the dark, steadying the trembling architecture of your body, reminding you that grief and anger are not diseases but vital, living responses. The herbs don’t demand you to be “better” or “healed” by dawn. They offer their bitter, grounding presence as you navigate the impossible terrain of loss.

Your jaw unclenches just enough to release a ragged sigh, a sound caught somewhere between a breath and a sob. The masseter muscle, so often your silent sentinel in moments of fury, softens a fraction. You cradle the nettle mug between your hands, its warmth seeping into your skin, and remember the medicine of the earth: nettle’s blood-cleansing promise is not a cure but a quiet assurance that you are held, even in the absence of answers.

The sky outside lightens, the first hues of dawn pale and tentative against the bruised night. Your body aches with the weight of anger and grief tangled together like sinew and bone. The tightness in your diaphragm lessens, just slightly, as you let the breath come in shallow waves, learning again how to be with this impossible pain.

You think of Pema Chödrön’s counsel to “lean into the sharp points,” to bear witness to the heart’s breaking rather than flinching away. This is not a moment for rushing forward or pulling back. It is a moment for presence, raw and unfiltered. You let your awareness rest on the muscles that hold your grief - the psoas clenched in fear, the trapezius and levator scapulae knotted with tension, the masseter locked in defiance. The body insists on being heard; it is not pathology, it is biology.

You recall van der Kolk’s insight that trauma needs a place to live safely within the body again, that the muscles must learn to loosen, the breath to deepen, the fascia to unknot. This is a slow, unrelenting work, and you are here for it, even in the dark and cold hours when anger feels too vast to hold.

Your hands tremble again, fingertips brushing the fibers of your cotton sheets, the soft worn fabric a tether to the present. You sip the last of the hawthorn tea, the bittersweet warmth pooling in your chest like a fragile ember. The muscles in your neck protest as you tilt your head, but you meet the ache with quiet tenderness. You are not alone in this. Your body remembers what your mind cannot yet say, and the herbs, the breath, the teachers - Pema, van der Kolk, Karla McLaren - stand with you in the shaking silence.

The anger at God, the betrayal, the rage that scorches through your muscles and bones - this is not shameful. It is the body’s fierce prayer, a demand to be seen and held in all its raw, unyielding truth. You breathe into the diaphragm, soften the masseter, press your palms against the window, and let the medicine of earth and time cradle your breaking heart. The night still presses heavy, but you are here, alive in the ache, rooted in the body’s deep knowing.

You settle back against the cool headboard, the thin cotton sheet slipping against your skin like a whisper. It’s 5:06 a.m., and the sky outside is a bruised wash of purple and gray, the kind of dawn that hesitates, unsure whether to break or hold the dark a moment longer. Your body is still a battleground: the psoas clenches like a fist around your lumbar spine, the trapezius and levator scapulae hum with tension, and your masseter muscle twitches with the residue of clenched teeth. The diaphragm spasms, shallow and uneven, a reminder that your breath is still captive.

You know this isn’t about “fixing” the pain or “getting over” the anger. The teachers you trust - Thich Nhat Hanh, Resmaa Menakem - remind you that the body’s wisdom is not linear. Thich Nhat Hanh’s gentle insistence on mindfulness is not a demand to be calm; it is an invitation to be present. “Hello, my anger,” he suggests we say. “I know you are there. I will take good care of you.” This is not a spiritual bypass. It is a radical act of somatic hospitality. You place a hand over your heart, feeling the erratic thump against your ribs, and you whisper it to the darkness: Hello, my anger. I know you are there.

Resmaa Menakem’s work on somatic abolitionism echoes in the quiet room. He teaches that trauma is not just an event, but a retention of energy in the body’s tissues. The betrayal you feel - the anger at God for the death of your friend - is a profound rupture that your body is trying to metabolize. Menakem emphasizes the importance of settling the nervous system, not by ignoring the pain, but by grounding the body so it can hold the charge without shattering. Your anger is a high-voltage current, and your body needs to become a wider, stronger conduit.

You decide to try a short embodied practice, a way to widen the conduit. You slide down from the bed, your bare feet meeting the cold wood floor. You stand, feeling the solid earth beneath you, the grounding force that Menakem so often speaks of. You bring your awareness to your feet, pressing the four corners of each sole into the floor. You imagine roots extending from your heels, down through the floorboards, deep into the earth.

Next, you bring your attention to your jaw. The masseter muscle is still tight, a stubborn knot of resistance. You take a slow, deep breath in through your nose, feeling the diaphragm expand, pushing your belly out. As you exhale through your mouth, you let your jaw drop open, wider than is comfortable, stretching the tight fascia. You make a sound - a low, guttural sigh that vibrates in your throat. You do this three times, each exhale a deliberate release of the tension held in the jaw.

Then, you move to the shoulders. The trapezius and levator scapulae are screaming. You inhale and hike your shoulders up to your ears, squeezing the muscles as tight as you can, holding the tension for a count of three. Then, with a sharp exhale, you drop them completely, letting gravity pull them down. You repeat this, feeling the rush of blood and warmth returning to the stiffened tissues.

Finally, you place both hands on your belly, right over the psoas. You breathe into your hands, feeling the rise and fall. You whisper, “It is safe to feel this. It is safe to be angry.” The body responds with a shudder, a sudden release of tears that are hot and fast. The anger doesn’t vanish, but it shifts. It becomes less of a cage and more of a current, moving through you rather than trapping you.

This is the work. It is not glamorous. It is sweaty, tear-stained, and exhausting. But it is real. The body knows how to heal if you give it the space and the tools. The herbs - the motherwort, the hawthorn, the skullcap - are allies in this work, supporting the physical structures as they bear the weight of the emotional storm.

As the morning light finally breaks, casting long shadows across the floor, you sit back on the edge of the bed. The anger is still there, a heavy stone in your chest, but the edges are softer. The body has spoken, and you have listened. This is not pathology; this is biology. This is the fierce, beautiful, terrifying reality of being human and loving someone enough to be furious when they are gone.

## FAQ

**Q: Is it normal to feel physical pain when I am angry at God?**

**A:** Yes, absolutely. Anger and grief are not just emotional experiences; they are deeply somatic. When you experience a profound betrayal or loss, the body responds by activating the sympathetic nervous system. This can manifest as tightness in the jaw (masseter), tension in the neck and shoulders (trapezius and levator scapulae), and constriction in the chest and diaphragm. This is your body’s biological response to trauma, not a sign of pathology.

**Q: How can herbs help with this kind of anger?**

**A:** Herbs are serious medicine that can support the body’s physical structures as they process emotional trauma. Nervines like skullcap can help soothe an overactive nervous system, reducing muscle tension and anxiety. Heart tonics like hawthorn and motherwort can support the cardiovascular system, easing the physical sensation of a "broken heart" and helping to regulate the heart rate and blood pressure during moments of intense anger or panic.

**Q: I feel guilty for being angry at God. What should I do?**

**A:** Guilt is a common secondary emotion, often stemming from cultural or religious conditioning that tells us anger is "bad" or "unspiritual." However, as teachers like Karla McLaren emphasize, anger is a vital messenger indicating that a boundary has been crossed or an injustice has occurred. Acknowledging and feeling your anger is a necessary part of the grieving process. Suppressing it only traps the energy in your body.

**Q: What is a simple somatic practice I can do when the anger feels overwhelming?**

**A:** Grounding is essential. Stand with your bare feet on the floor, feeling the solid earth beneath you. Take deep breaths into your belly, allowing the diaphragm to fully expand. Consciously release tension in your jaw by letting your mouth hang open slightly on the exhale. You can also try the "shoulder shrug" practice: inhale and squeeze your shoulders to your ears, then exhale sharply and let them drop. This helps release trapped energy in the trapezius and levator scapulae.

**Q: Will this anger ever go away?**

**A:** The intensity of the anger will likely shift and change over time as you process the grief. It may not disappear entirely, but it can transform from a consuming fire into a manageable current. The goal is not to eliminate the anger, but to widen your body's capacity to hold it without being overwhelmed. As you continue to listen to your body and support it with somatic practices and herbal allies, the anger will integrate into your larger experience of love and loss.

---

<section data-author="byline">
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality.
Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.169Z">May 28, 2026</time>.
In our work on anger at god for the death of a friend, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
</section>
